description:
Integration-developer scenario – verifying a partner’s detached signature and then safeguarding the verified
    clear-text with symmetric GPG encryption.

    1.  Pre-existing material
        a)  /home/user/work/integration/message.txt  
            ASCII text, UTF-8, exactly three lines (including the final trailing line-feed):  
                Line 1:  POST /v1/payments  
                Line 2:  X-Correlation-ID: 123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000  
                Line 3:  {"amount":42}  

        b)  /home/user/work/integration/message.txt.sig – partner’s detached signature  
        c)  /home/user/work/integration/partner_pub.asc  – partner’s ASCII-armoured public key  

    2.  What you must do in the terminal  
        Step-1  Import the partner’s public key into your personal key-ring.  
        Step-2  Verify the detached signature against message.txt.  
                • Redirect (» not overwrite) the *combined* stdout **and** stderr stream
                  produced by the `gpg --verify …` command into the file  
                  /home/user/work/integration/verification.log  
                  Use the exact GNU-coreutils redirection syntax that merges both streams so
                  the file contains exactly what was displayed in the terminal.  
        Step-3  Perform symmetric encryption of message.txt with AES256 producing  
                  /home/user/work/integration/message.txt.gpg  
                  • Use the literal pass-phrase Integration123  
                  • The file must be written in the same directory; no additional extension
                    other than “.gpg”.  
                  • Ensure the command runs non-interactively (batch mode, no TTY prompts).

    3.  Required verification.log format (the automated test will check this):
        • The file must exist at /home/user/work/integration/verification.log  
        • It must contain **at least** one line with the exact substring  
              gpg: Signature made  
          (capitalisation must match).  
        • It must contain **at least** one line with the exact substring  
              gpg: Good signature  
          (capitalisation must match).  
        Do not add any extra user-crafted lines; write only the raw `gpg` output.

    4.  Required state at the end
        • /home/user/work/integration/verification.log  exists and satisfies §3 above.  
        • /home/user/work/integration/message.txt.gpg   exists and is a valid GPG
          symmetric ciphertext (binary or ASCII-armoured is acceptable).  
        • The original clear-text and signature files remain untouched.  

    5.  Important constraints  
        • Work only inside /home/user/work/integration/ – do **not** move files elsewhere.  
        • No root privileges are available; standard user commands only.  
        • Use GnuPG’s command-line tool `gpg` (already present in the container).  
        • Accomplish the three steps with as few simple commands as you deem fit; complexity
          is intentionally minimal.  
        • Print the normal console output of your commands, but do **not** print the contents
          of the encrypted file or the pass-phrase.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful completion:

    1.  Path: /home/user/work/integration/verification.log  
        Must exist and be a regular text file readable by user.  
        Mandatory content checks (substring-based, order doesn’t matter):  
            a) “gpg: Signature made”  
            b) “gpg: Good signature”  

    2.  Path: /home/user/work/integration/message.txt.gpg  
        Must exist and be non-empty.  
        The first eight bytes must match either  
            0x8C 0x0D …      (binary OpenPGP packet, typical)  
            or the ASCII armour preamble “-----BEGIN PGP MESSAGE-----”.  

    3.  Original files remain:  
            /home/user/work/integration/message.txt  
            /home/user/work/integration/message.txt.sig  
            /home/user/work/integration/partner_pub.asc  

    4.  Key-ring: the imported partner key’s primary UID is  
            Partner API <partner@example.com>  
        (GPG will show this string in the “Good signature” line).  

    Note for automated tests:  
      • `grep -q "gpg: Signature made" verification.log && grep -q "gpg: Good signature" verification.log`  
        is sufficient to validate the log.  
      • `file message.txt.gpg` should return “GPG encrypted data”.
name: task_000000_e31ac043
